title: Printing inherited classes using operator<<

exercises.title: Polymorphic printing via virtual print
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement the virtual `print` pattern so that `operator<<` produces the correct output for both `Shape` and `Circle` objects when printed through a `Shape&` reference.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (1-50 characters, no spaces).
- Line 2: a double `radius`.

## Output

Exactly three lines:
1. `[Shape: <name>]`
2. `[Circle: <name>, r=<radius>]`
3. `[Circle: <name>, r=<radius>]`

The radius is printed with default `std::cout` formatting (no fixed precision).

Line 1 prints a `Shape` directly. Line 2 prints a `Circle` directly. Line 3 prints the same `Circle` through a `const Shape&` reference, demonstrating polymorphic dispatch.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Shape` with a protected `std::string m_name` member and a constructor taking `const std::string&`.
- `Shape` has a public `virtual void print(std::ostream& out) const` that writes `[Shape: <name>]` to `out`.
- `Shape` has a `friend std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& out, const Shape& s)` that calls `s.print(out)` and returns `out`.
- `Shape` has a `virtual ~Shape() = default`.
- Define `Circle` publicly derived from `Shape` with a `double m_radius` member. Its constructor takes `const std::string&` and `double`.
- `Circle` overrides `print` (use `override`) to write `[Circle: <name>, r=<radius>]`.
- In `main`, read the name and radius. Create a `Shape`, print it. Create a `Circle`, print it directly, then print it through a `const Shape&` reference.

exercises.title: Polymorphic employee directory
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build an `Employee` hierarchy where `Manager` and `Engineer` override a virtual `print` function. Store mixed employees in a `std::vector<Employee*>` and print them all through `operator<<`, demonstrating that the correct derived output appears without any per-class `operator<<` overloads.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10).
- Next `N` lines: either `manager <name> <dept>` or `engineer <name> <lang>`, where `<name>`, `<dept>`, and `<lang>` are single words (no spaces).

## Output

For each employee, one line:
- Manager: `Manager: <name>, dept=<dept>`
- Engineer: `Engineer: <name>, lang=<lang>`

Followed by a final line:
```
Total: <N>
```

## Requirements

- Define a class `Employee` with a protected `std::string m_name`, a constructor taking `const std::string&`, a `virtual ~Employee() = default`, a `virtual void print(std::ostream& out) const` that writes `Employee: <name>`, and a `friend operator<<` that calls `print`.
- Define `Manager` (publicly derived from `Employee`) with a `std::string m_dept` member. Constructor takes name and dept. Override `print` (use `override`) to write `Manager: <name>, dept=<dept>`.
- Define `Engineer` (publicly derived from `Employee`) with a `std::string m_lang` member. Constructor takes name and lang. Override `print` to write `Engineer: <name>, lang=<lang>`.
- In `main`, read N employees into a `std::vector<Employee*>` using `new`.
- Loop over the vector printing each employee with `std::cout << *ptr << '\n'`.
- Print `Total: <N>`.
- Delete all dynamically allocated employees.

test_cases.label: Mixed manager and engineer
test_cases.stdin:
3
manager Alice Sales
engineer Bob C++
manager Carol HR
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Manager: Alice, dept=Sales
Engineer: Bob, lang=C++
Manager: Carol, dept=HR
Total: 3

test_cases.label: All engineers
test_cases.stdin:
2
engineer Dave Python
engineer Eve Rust
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Engineer: Dave, lang=Python
Engineer: Eve, lang=Rust
Total: 2
